articles.title: Darfur’s Burn Scars Are Starting to Read Like a Food Map
articles.summary: Satellite analysis around El Fasher, market monitors and aid-route reporting point to the same hard pattern: villages burned, farmers scattered, roads taxed or blocked, and famine following the paths where food can no longer move.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Darfur’s Burn Scars Are Starting to Read Like a Food Map. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Darfur’s Burn Scars Are Starting to Read Like a Food Map
articles.visual_archetype: institutional room or workplace scene with people, tools, and context
articles.visual_brief: Inside a modest humanitarian analysis room in Port Sudan or Nairobi, two food-security analysts and a logistics officer stand around a crowded table covered with printed satellite images of burned settlements near El Fasher, convoy-route sheets, market-price charts, handwritten locality lists, sticky notes, a laptop with a muted map interface, a radio handset and a thermos of tea. Fluorescent office light, worn chairs, a taped wall calendar, stacked field notebooks and half-open file boxes should make the room feel practical and overworked. The composition should focus on hands pointing at crop-field printouts, road-access notes and commodity-price tables, not on a heroic screen or abstract map glow.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Darfur’s Burn Scars Are Starting to Read Like a Food Map
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Darfur’s Burn Scars Are Starting to Read Like a Food Map. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: Nairobi’s Drains Are Running Out of Room
articles.summary: March floods exposed more than one bad storm. As Kenya’s long rains keep testing roads, riverbanks and clinics, Nairobi’s culverts are carrying a bigger question for fast-growing cities across East Africa.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Nairobi’s Drains Are Running Out of Room. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Nairobi’s Drains Are Running Out of Room
articles.visual_archetype: wide environmental scene showing infrastructure, landscape, or atmosphere
articles.visual_brief: A wide, documentary-style environmental scene in Nairobi after heavy rain: a swollen brown drainage channel in the foreground, water pushing around concrete culverts and debris screens, roadside stalls and matatus on a raised road, pedestrians keeping to the edges, low clouds over the city skyline, and informal housing and green riverbank visible along the channel. Natural overcast light, human-scale but not sensational, emphasizing drainage infrastructure, landscape, and atmosphere.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Nairobi’s Drains Are Running Out of Room
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Nairobi’s Drains Are Running Out of Room. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: 3.98 Million Home Sales Turn Spring Open Houses Into Math Class
articles.summary: NAR’s March report shows a market with more listings, lower rates than last spring and still too few homes ordinary paychecks can reach. The spring ritual is back, but every sidewalk tour now comes with a calculator.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: 3.98 Million Home Sales Turn Spring Open Houses Into Math Class. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: 3.98 Million Home Sales Turn Spring Open Houses Into Math Class
articles.visual_archetype: documentary street-level scene with motion and local texture
articles.visual_brief: A documentary street-level spring homebuying scene in a dense U.S. neighborhood: several prospective buyers in light jackets walking past a modest open-house sign on a busy sidewalk, one person checking a phone mortgage calculator, another holding a folded listing sheet, a real estate agent half-visible at a rowhouse doorway, parked cars, stoops, flyers, budding street trees, curbside trash bins and pedestrians in the background; late-afternoon natural light, candid motion blur, local texture and no charts, no maps, no courthouse, no symbolic scales, no state silhouettes.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: 3.98 Million Home Sales Turn Spring Open Houses Into Math Class
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: 3.98 Million Home Sales Turn Spring Open Houses Into Math Class. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: NDIS Autism Test Asks Whether Help Can Beat the Label
articles.summary: Australia’s plan to move many young autistic children from individual NDIS packages into Thriving Kids is a budget fight with a family-life test: diagnosis can unlock help, but it can also jam the doorway.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: NDIS Autism Test Asks Whether Help Can Beat the Label. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: NDIS Autism Test Asks Whether Help Can Beat the Label

articles.visual_brief: A documentary street-level scene outside a suburban Australian early-learning centre at morning drop-off: parents steering strollers and scooters along a wet footpath, a crossing guard in a fluorescent vest, a child’s backpack with a dangling toy, a speech therapist’s canvas tote partly visible, gum trees and small shopfronts in the background, natural overcast light, slight motion blur from passing bicycles, no logos emphasized, humane and observational rather than staged.

articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: NDIS Autism Test Asks Whether Help Can Beat the Label. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: Blue Proxy Card Puts Burbank’s Back Lot in a Streaming Fight
articles.summary: Warner Bros. Discovery shareholders have approved Paramount Skydance’s bid. The next question is what a studio gate, a streaming menu, a newsroom wall and a movie worker’s call sheet look like under one owner.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Blue Proxy Card Puts Burbank’s Back Lot in a Streaming Fight. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.visual_brief: A close, shallow-focus still life on a studio office desk: a blue proxy card partly tucked under a brass Warner-style lot key tag, a pencil with voting checkmarks, a folded call sheet, and two small streaming remote controls at the edge of frame. Warm afternoon light through blinds, faint coffee ring, no logos copied exactly, no people, no maps, documentary realism with crisp paper texture.

articles.title: Wichita’s One Robot Dog Has a July Rulebook Test
articles.summary: A 4-3 council vote gave Wichita police one four-legged robot, not two. Now the city has a July 7 deadline to show what Spot will record, when it will walk, and whether a strange-looking safety tool can be governed before it becomes ordinary police gear.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Wichita’s One Robot Dog Has a July Rulebook Test. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Wichita’s One Robot Dog Has a July Rulebook Test
articles.visual_archetype: ground-level human scene with a specific setting and natural light
articles.visual_brief: Ground-level documentary scene inside Wichita City Hall on a bright weekday morning: residents sit on wooden benches outside the council chamber, a city agenda packet open on someone’s lap to a police technology item, a uniformed officer speaks quietly with a council staffer near the doorway, and natural light from tall windows falls across polished floors. No futuristic glow, no weapons, no maps; the mood is civic, practical, and a little odd.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Wichita’s One Robot Dog Has a July Rulebook Test
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Wichita’s One Robot Dog Has a July Rulebook Test. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: Baidoa’s Water Trucks Keep Moving While Geneva’s Ledger Lags
articles.summary: IOM says drought has displaced nearly 62,000 people in five Somali districts since January. The harder test is whether water, food and clinics can reach families before a rural drought becomes another long urban emergency.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Baidoa’s Water Trucks Keep Moving While Geneva’s Ledger Lags. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Baidoa’s Water Trucks Keep Moving While Geneva’s Ledger Lags
articles.visual_archetype: hands-at-work scene focused on process, craft, or labor
articles.visual_brief: Close, documentary-style image of two aid workers’ hands at the rear valve of a dusty water truck in Baidoa, one hand tightening a metal coupling while the other steadies a thick hose as water splashes into yellow jerrycans lined on red earth. Background softly shows women waiting, a mechanic’s wrench on the truck step, heat haze, and canvas shelters, but the focus stays on the hands, hose, valve, and practical labor of keeping water moving.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Baidoa’s Water Trucks Keep Moving While Geneva’s Ledger Lags
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Baidoa’s Water Trucks Keep Moving While Geneva’s Ledger Lags. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: IMF’s 19% Energy Shock Reaches the Checkout Line
articles.summary: The IMF’s April outlook begins with war, fuel and a shipping choke point. Its clearest money test is more ordinary: how long one price shock can keep traveling through gas receipts, freight bills, fertilizer invoices, food budgets, interest-rate decisions and government debt.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: IMF’s 19% Energy Shock Reaches the Checkout Line. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: IMF’s 19% Energy Shock Reaches the Checkout Line
articles.visual_archetype: abstract physical installation using real-world objects, not maps or scales
articles.visual_brief: Build a tabletop installation on matte gray concrete: a long black rubber hose curves through a chain of everyday objects, beginning at a brass fuel nozzle, then threading past a tiny burlap sack of white fertilizer granules, a folded freight invoice, a torn grocery receipt, a loaf of bread, a bus pass, a mortgage envelope and a desk calendar opened to April 2026. Dust each object lightly with graphite powder, as if one dark price trail brushed across all of them. Use soft side light, shallow depth of field and tactile shadows. No maps, no scales, no flags, no screens, no charts, no state outlines.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: IMF’s 19% Energy Shock Reaches the Checkout Line
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: IMF’s 19% Energy Shock Reaches the Checkout Line. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: CDC’s 3,606,400 Birth Certificates Turn Family Math Into Public Policy
articles.summary: NCHS’s 2025 birth report looks modest: 1% fewer births, teen births at another low, preterm births stuck and C-sections up. Read closely, it is a family-life ledger about timing, money, hospitals and choice.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: CDC’s 3,606,400 Birth Certificates Turn Family Math Into Public Policy. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: CDC’s 3,606,400 Birth Certificates Turn Family Math Into Public Policy
articles.visual_archetype: abstract physical installation using real-world objects, not maps or scales
articles.visual_brief: Create a tabletop installation in soft window light: a neat stack of blank birth-certificate forms, 36 tiny wooden blocks stamped with digits from 3,606,400, a paper hospital bracelet looped around a pencil, a folded surgical cap, a small pair of knitted booties, a calendar page marked April 2026, a childcare invoice, and a grocery receipt curling at the edge. Arrange the objects like a quiet ledger, with no people, no maps, no scales, no medical gore, no partisan colors, and no state silhouettes. The scene should feel concrete, humane and slightly playful, with the federal-style document stack anchoring the composition.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: CDC’s 3,606,400 Birth Certificates Turn Family Math Into Public Policy
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: CDC’s 3,606,400 Birth Certificates Turn Family Math Into Public Policy. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: Koyo Kouoh’s Team Lowers Venice’s Volume
articles.summary: Less than two weeks before the 61st Venice Biennale opens, In Minor Keys has become a test of attention, mourning, national image-making and how much politics a global art event can keep outside the door.

articles.title: Cape Cod’s Blue-Blood Quota Retreat Leaves a Crab Test Unsettled
articles.summary: Massachusetts backed away from giving biomedical firms more horseshoe crabs this spring. The stranger, larger question remains: how fast can medicine move from a wild animal’s blue blood to animal-free endotoxin tests without gambling with drug safety, shorebirds or fishing towns?
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Cape Cod’s Blue-Blood Quota Retreat Leaves a Crab Test Unsettled. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Cape Cod’s Blue-Blood Quota Retreat Leaves a Crab Test Unsettled
articles.visual_archetype: macro material-detail scene where texture carries the story
articles.visual_brief: Extreme close-up at low tide on a Cape Cod or Delaware Bay beach: wet tan sand, tiny greenish horseshoe crab eggs mixed with shell grit, the ribbed edge of a dark horseshoe crab shell entering one corner, a few translucent bubbles and a faint blue reflection in a shallow puddle. Natural overcast light, tactile detail, no people, no maps, no lab vials.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Cape Cod’s Blue-Blood Quota Retreat Leaves a Crab Test Unsettled
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Cape Cod’s Blue-Blood Quota Retreat Leaves a Crab Test Unsettled. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: NPT’s glass booths hear a battered bargain in New York
articles.summary: The 11th NPT review conference opened at UN Headquarters with 191 parties, no consensus document since 2010, New START expired and IAEA inspectors missing continuity on parts of Iran’s declared inventory. The test is whether the world’s biggest nuclear bargain can still do daily work.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: NPT’s glass booths hear a battered bargain in New York. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: NPT’s glass booths hear a battered bargain in New York
articles.visual_archetype: architectural detail that reveals power, constraint, or neglect
articles.visual_brief: Close architectural-detail image inside a United Nations conference room: a row of glass interpretation booths above the chamber, one booth light glowing, tangled black headsets on a narrow ledge, a worn brass room-number plate, blue carpet and blurred country placards far below. The mood should feel formal but strained, with fluorescent light, fingerprints on glass and the machinery of diplomacy visible rather than heroic.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: NPT’s glass booths hear a battered bargain in New York
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: NPT’s glass booths hear a battered bargain in New York. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: 32BJ’s $4.50 Raise Puts New York’s Lobby Math on the Desk
articles.summary: A tentative deal for nearly 34,000 doormen, porters, supers and resident managers avoided a citywide strike. Now the money question moves from Park Avenue to paychecks, pensions, maintenance fees and rent ledgers.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: 32BJ’s $4.50 Raise Puts New York’s Lobby Math on the Desk. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.visual_archetype: nonliteral atmospheric scene with color, depth, and one surprising focal object
articles.visual_brief: A moody, cinematic apartment lobby after dusk, with warm brass, deep teal shadows and a sense of depth down a polished corridor. Mail carts, bundled recycling and a half-open package room blur in the background. In the foreground, one surprising focal object sits sharply lit on a marble concierge desk: a single bright orange strike whistle beside a polished key fob and a folded payroll stub. No people, no signs, no maps; the image should feel like the quiet pause after a city almost had to learn how its buildings work.

articles.title: Nebraska’s Candy Waiver Puts SNAP at the Checkout Test
articles.summary: Nebraska wants USDA to add candy to its SNAP no-buy list after soda and energy drinks were barred Jan. 1. The fight is not just about one sweet aisle; it is about health, dignity, store software, lawsuits and who gets to define a normal grocery cart.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Nebraska’s Candy Waiver Puts SNAP at the Checkout Test. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Nebraska’s Candy Waiver Puts SNAP at the Checkout Test
articles.visual_archetype: nonliteral atmospheric scene with color, depth, and one surprising focal object
articles.visual_brief: A moody, shallow-depth-of-field grocery checkout scene at night, seen from counter height: a glowing card reader, blurred rows of bright drink cans and candy colors in the background, and one surprising focal object in crisp detail — a small paper receipt curling upward with a single red checkout-stamp mark. No people’s faces, no political symbols, no maps; cinematic teal and warm amber lighting, humane and atmospheric rather than accusatory.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Nebraska’s Candy Waiver Puts SNAP at the Checkout Test
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Nebraska’s Candy Waiver Puts SNAP at the Checkout Test. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: FCC’s April 22 Notice Puts the Kids-TV Rating Icon on the Couch
articles.summary: The Media Bureau has not changed a single rating. It has asked whether TV-Y, TV-Y7 and TV-G programs need new treatment when they include gender-identity themes, and that question now runs from a Washington docket to app menus, standards meetings, parent settings and one child’s next show.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: FCC’s April 22 Notice Puts the Kids-TV Rating Icon on the Couch. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: FCC’s April 22 Notice Puts the Kids-TV Rating Icon on the Couch
articles.visual_archetype: domestic-scale scene showing how a policy or system reaches daily life
articles.visual_brief: A warm, realistic living-room scene in early evening: a low coffee table with a TV remote, two half-finished cereal bowls, a folded school worksheet, a parent’s phone open to a streaming profile settings screen, and a paper note with a four-digit PIN. In the softly blurred background, a television home screen shows a small TV-Y7 rating icon beside a children’s cartoon tile. No politicians, no maps, no courtroom imagery, no giant symbols; the scene should feel ordinary, domestic and slightly tense, showing how a federal media-policy question reaches household routine.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: FCC’s April 22 Notice Puts the Kids-TV Rating Icon on the Couch
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: FCC’s April 22 Notice Puts the Kids-TV Rating Icon on the Couch. AI-generated image by The Press.
